metadata.title: Cash Conversion Cycle: $323M Locked in Transit
metadata.note: CLI video. CCC analysis for Halverson Manufacturing.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.
Halverson Manufacturing has 323 million dollars locked in the operating cycle. That is more than its total debt. A 10-day improvement in the cash conversion cycle releases 38 million dollars in cash.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- CCC = DSO + DIO - DPO. 323M locked. 10-day improvement = 38M released.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Halverson Manufacturing has 323 million dollars locked in the operating cycle. That is more than its total debt. A 10-day improvement in the cash conversion cyc

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to compute the cash conversion cycle from DSO, DIO, and DPO for three years and estimate capital locked.

props.command: claude "Compute cash conversion cycle: DSO=[60,58,62], DIO=[90,92,88], DPO=[45,47,43] for years 2021-2023. COGS=$1.1B. Compute CCC, capital locked (CCC*COGS/365), and cash released by a 10-day CCC improvement."

beats.narration_text: The script computes DSO plus DIO minus DPO for each year, multiplies by COGS per day to get capital locked, and estimates the release from a 10-day CCC improvement.

props.code:
COGS=1.1e9; d=COGS/365
data=[
  (2021,60,90,45),
  (2022,58,92,47),
  (2023,62,88,43),
]
for yr,dso,dio,dpo in data:
    ccc=dso+dio-dpo
    locked=ccc*d/1e6
    release=10*d/1e6
    print(f"{yr}: CCC={ccc}d  "
          f"Locked=${locked:.0f}M  "
          f"10d-release=${release:.0f}M")

beats.narration_text: Three horizontal timeline bars show DSO in teal, DIO in amber, and DPO clawing back in green. Each bar ends at the CCC endpoint with the capital locked labeled.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_CCCTimeline: 3-year horizontal bar chart showing DSO/DIO/DPO composition and CCC endpoint.

beats.narration_text: 10-day CCC improvement equals 38 million released. No banker required.

props.command: claude "Which component is worst: DSO or DIO? Benchmarks: industrial DSO=45d, DIO=65d. Compute the gap vs. benchmark and the dollar value of closing each gap."

beats.narration_text: DIO is 25 days above the industrial benchmark. Closing that gap alone releases 82 million dollars. The CCC is an operational balance sheet hiding in plain sight.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- DIO 25d above benchmark. Closing DIO gap = $82M released. CCC is the hidden balance sheet.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: DIO is 25 days above the industrial benchmark. Closing that gap alone releases 82 million dollars. The CCC is an operational balance sheet hiding in plain sight

beats.narration_text: Every day of CCC improvement is 3 million dollars. The cash is already earned. It is just sitting in the pipeline.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- every day = $3M. Cash is earned. It is in the pipeline.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Every day of CCC improvement is 3 million dollars. The cash is already earned. It is just sitting in the pipeline.

beats.narration_text: Next: compare CCC to industry peers. Find the benchmark and compute the value of closing the gap. Follow for more corporate finance teardowns.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps: peer benchmarking of CCC. Close the gap, release the cash.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Next: compare CCC to industry peers. Find the benchmark and compute the value of closing the gap. Follow for more corporate finance teardowns.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
